Plots

Plots regarding BMI/obesity:

The National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) is a questionnaire administered by the US Centers for Disease Control (CDC) that is meant to study diet and health in U.S. adults. Specifically, the NHANES results can be categorized into a dataset where each row represents one adult participant, and columns indicating various health markers such as age, obesity, nutrient consumption, etc. As such, the NHANES dataset provides ways to explore how various diet quality and/or socioeconomic factors affect BMI and obesity.

library(readr)
library(dplyr)

Attaching package: 'dplyr'
The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    filter, lag
The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
library(ggplot2)
data = read_csv("nhanes.csv")
Rows: 3787 Columns: 26
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr  (9): sex, race, education, marriage, cant_afford_meals, food_security, ...
dbl (17): id, age, income_to_poverty, household_size, bmi, is_obese, fiber_d...

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
ggplot(data, aes(x=age, y=bmi)) +
  geom_point(color="darkblue") +
  labs(title="No clear trends between participant age and BMI", x="Participant Age", y="BMI") +
  theme_minimal()
ggplot(data, aes(x=bmi)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth=5, fill="darkblue", color="white") +
  labs(title="Distribution of recorded participant BMIs", x="BMI") +
  theme_minimal()
ggplot(data, aes(x=bmi, fill=sex)) +
  geom_histogram(position="stack", color="white", binwidth=2) +
  labs(title="Distribution of recorded participant BMIs", x="BMI", subtitle="Unimodal, slightly skewed right") +
  theme_minimal()
ggplot(data, aes(x=health_condition, y=bmi)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)) +
  labs(title="No clear trend between health condition and BMI", x="Health Condition", y="BMI")
library(broom)
model = lm(bmi ~ kcal, data=data)
model_int = model[[1]][[1]]
model_slope = model[[1]][[2]]
r2 = glance(model)[[1]][[1]]

ggplot(data, aes(x=kcal, y=bmi)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_abline(slope = model_slope, intercept = model_int, color="red") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title="There does not seem to be a linear relationship between kcal intake and BMI", subtitle="Line of best fit r^2 = 0.0005", x="Energy Intake (kcal)", y= "BMI")

The following graphs will attempt to find a suitable subset to focus visualizations to. They may or may not use code that has not been covered in this class yet.

data |>
  ggplot(aes(x=kcal, y=bmi)) +
    geom_point(color="darkblue", size=0.5) +
    facet_wrap(~ health_condition) +
    theme_minimal()
data |>
  ggplot(aes(x=kcal, y=bmi)) +
    geom_point(color="darkblue", size=0.5) +
    facet_wrap(~ race) +
    theme_minimal()
data |>
  ggplot(aes(x=health_location)) +
    geom_bar() +
    facet_wrap(~ is_obese) +
    theme_minimal() + 
    theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1))
data |>
  ggplot(aes(y=kcal, x=bmi)) +
    #geom_bar(position="dodge") +
    geom_point() +
    facet_wrap(~ health_location) +
    theme_minimal()
data |>
  ggplot(aes(x=bmi)) +
    geom_bar() +
    facet_wrap(~ education) + #college grad?? stronger peak there
    theme_minimal()
data |> filter(education == "College graduate or above") |>
  filter(health_location == "A doctor's office or health center") |>
  ggplot(aes(x=bmi)) +
    geom_bar() +
    facet_wrap(~ food_security)+
    theme_minimal()

Use this cell to test mutations on the dataset and observe corrs to a variable

corr_search_data = data |>
  filter(health_condition == "Good" | health_condition == "Very Good" | health_condition == "Excellent") |>
  filter(income_to_poverty >= 1) |>
  filter(income_to_poverty <= 3) |>
  filter(health_location == "A doctor's office or health center") |>
  filter(is_obese == 1) |>
  filter(education == "College graduate or above" | education == "Some college or AA degree") |>
  filter(age <= 33)

lin_outcome = "bmi"
cor_vars = names(corr_search_data)
cor_vecs = rep(0, length(cor_vars))
for (i in c(1:length(cor_vars))) { #so we run cor() on every variable we have
  if (is.numeric(corr_search_data[[cor_vars[i]]])) {
    cor_vecs[i] = cor(corr_search_data[[lin_outcome]], corr_search_data[[cor_vars[i]]])
    #print(paste0("cor(", lin_outcome, ", ", cor_vars[i], "): ", cor_vecs[i]))
  } else {
    cor_vecs[i]=0
  }
}
Warning in cor(corr_search_data[[lin_outcome]],
corr_search_data[[cor_vars[i]]]): the standard deviation is zero
data.frame(x=cor_vars, corr=cor_vecs) |>
  mutate(r2 = corr ** 2) |>
  filter(x != "id") |>
  filter(x != "bmi") |>
  filter(x != "is_obese") |>
  arrange(desc(r2)) #well thats not promising
                    x        corr           r2
1          sodium_den  0.56014358 0.3137608323
2        sugar_intake -0.47725603 0.2277733199
3           sugar_den -0.46572387 0.2168987198
4         carb_intake -0.39654488 0.1572478458
5      household_size  0.29036953 0.0843144644
6                 age  0.28911964 0.0835901669
7          satfat_den  0.21669027 0.0469546714
8       sodium_intake  0.19428211 0.0377455391
9                kcal -0.16985376 0.0288502983
10       fiber_intake -0.15824038 0.0250400163
11  income_to_poverty -0.10365902 0.0107451932
12     protein_intake  0.07162221 0.0051297403
13          fiber_den -0.05107565 0.0026087216
14         fat_intake -0.02105273 0.0004432174
15                sex  0.00000000 0.0000000000
16               race  0.00000000 0.0000000000
17          education  0.00000000 0.0000000000
18           marriage  0.00000000 0.0000000000
19  cant_afford_meals  0.00000000 0.0000000000
20      food_security  0.00000000 0.0000000000
21   health_condition  0.00000000 0.0000000000
22    health_location  0.00000000 0.0000000000
23 seen_mental_health  0.00000000 0.0000000000
students = data |>
  filter(health_condition == "Good" | health_condition == "Very Good" | health_condition == "Excellent") |>
  filter(income_to_poverty >= 1) |>
  filter(income_to_poverty < 4) |>
  filter(health_location == "A doctor's office or health center") |>
  filter(is_obese == 1) |>
  filter(education == "College graduate or above" | education == "Some college or AA degree") |>
  filter(age <= 33)

sm = lm(bmi ~ sodium_den, data=students)
sm_int = sm[[1]][[1]]
sm_slope = sm[[1]][[2]]

print(paste0("# of participants: ", dim(students)[1], " // R^2: ", round(glance(sm)[1][[1]], 2) ))
[1] "# of participants: 23 // R^2: 0.16"
ggplot(students, aes(x=sodium_den, y=bmi)) +
  geom_point(color="darkblue") +
  labs(title="Obese adults who were under 33 and a student with Good or better health...", subtitle="...who visits a doctor's office or health center with moderate income (1-3x poverty line)", x="Sodium Density in g/1000 kcal", y="BMI") +
  geom_abline(slope = sm_slope, intercept = sm_int, color="red") +
  theme_minimal()

Extra EDA

ggplot(data, aes(x=race, fill=education)) +
  geom_bar(position="dodge") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)) +
  labs(title="There are many more Non-Hispanic White College Grad participants")
ggplot(data, aes(x=health_condition)) +
  geom_bar(fill="darkblue") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)) +
  labs(title="There are many more participants in Good or Very Good health")
ggplot(data, aes(x=sex, fill=marriage)) +
  geom_bar() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title="More female participants, and most participants overall are married")
#somewhat broken
#ggplot(filtered_data, aes(x=income_to_poverty, y=health_location)) +
#  geom_boxplot() +
#  theme_minimal() +
#  #theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)) +
#  labs(title="Income to Poverty Ratio might be correlated to Healthcare Location")
ggplot(data, aes(x=is_obese, fill=sex)) +
  geom_bar(position="dodge") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title="More people are not obese, and females are more represented in either case", x="Is Obese? (0=no, 1=yes)")
